paragraphs.text: ECOM REPLY TO DFA PUBLIC CONSULTATION
24 October 2025
ECOMERCE EUROPE
fse

Ecommerce Europe welcomes the opportunity to provide input to the European Commission's Digital Fairness Act. Ecommerce Europe is the united voice of the European Digital Commerce sector, representing the interests of companies selling goods and services online to consumers in Europe. Our mission is to act at EU level by engaging with policymakers to create a better regulatory framework for all e-merchants.

section_path: Ecommerce Europe Reply to Digital Fairness Act Public Consultation

paragraphs.text: Given our role in the digital ecosystem, we find ourselves at the receiving end of many of the potential changes currently being discussed. We are convinced that the existing EU legal framework already provides a comprehensive toolbox that would address the list of problematic practices identified by the Commission. The main challenge today is not the absence of rules, but their consistent enforcement and interpretation across Member States. We firmly believe the Digital Fairness Act is neither necessary nor proportionate.

section_path: Ecommerce Europe Reply to Digital Fairness Act Public Consultation

paragraphs.text: In the paragraphs below we explain why this is the case and why we argue against the introduction of the Digital Fairness Act. Another layer of legislation would only increase complexity and regulatory fragmentation without addressing the issues identified in the Fitness Check. Instead, the Commission should focus on enhancing enforcement, improving cooperation between national authorities, and clarifying how existing laws apply to new practices. The issue that demands attention is that of uneven and inconsistent enforcement of rules.

section_path: Ecommerce Europe Reply to Digital Fairness Act Public Consultation

paragraphs.text: In recent years, the European Commission (EC) embarked on a mission to redesign the way in which the Digital Single Market works. Measures such as the Digital Services Act (DSA), the Digital Markets Act (DMA), the AI Act, the Omnibus Directive (Directive (EU) 2019/2161) and the different legal frameworks on data protection and usage of data demonstrate the commitment of the regulators to ensure a high level of protection for consumers, as well as establishing a framework in which businesses can operate fairly.

section_path: Ecommerce Europe Reply to Digital Fairness Act Public Consultation

paragraphs.text: This flurry of measures also comes at a cost, and that is implementation and patience. Not only businesses are asking for a pause on further regulatory measures, but so are authorities. Companies need time to catch up with implementation and so do the Member States. It is not a coincidence that the Omnibus Directive was only fully implemented in May 2024, whereas in the case of the Digital Services Act several Member States have yet to fully do so. On top of this, the famous Draghi and Letta reports have repeatedly stated the need to press pause on rule making and focus on rule implementing. On this basis, the European Commission has adopted a simplification agenda, which includes initiatives such as the Digital Omnibus.

section_path: Ecommerce Europe Reply to Digital Fairness Act Public Consultation

paragraphs.text: Due to over-regulation and lack of coherence between the rules as well as between the Member States when implementing said rules, we suffer from regulatory overlap and fragmentation. These fundamental issues hinder the development of the Single Market and inhibit a predictable regulatory environment. For example, the Fitness Check takes note of overlaps given "the parallel application of EU consumer law in

section_path: Ecommerce Europe Reply to Digital Fairness Act Public Consultation

paragraphs.text: conjunction with other digital laws concerning similar or exactly the same commercial practices by the same traders". As for regulatory fragmentation and its prevention, it is again recognised in the Fitness Check as an issue which must be overcome in order to reduce "legal uncertainty for market participants about the application of EU consumer law to practices in the digital environment". Yet, there is a strong push for more legislation that would further perpetuate the issues previously highlighted.

section_path: Ecommerce Europe Reply to Digital Fairness Act Public Consultation

paragraphs.text: Questions regarding the evidence base and cost estimates were raised even by the Regulatory Scrutiny Board (RSB) when they issued their own opinion on the Fitness Check. The RSB highlighted that opinion-based data "as well as the difficulties in isolating and attributing the impacts directly to the Directives" are not sufficiently explained in the main report and for this reason the conclusions should not go beyond the empirical analysis, and yet the conclusions do. The opinion further states the need to develop the "evidence regarding identified problematic practices". Lastly, regarding the consumer detriment aspect, the Fitness Check fails at explaining the limitation of the opinion data (perceived problems) "as a proxy for complaint incidence rate".

section_path: Ecommerce Europe Reply to Digital Fairness Act Public Consultation

paragraphs.text: Echoing these views, we reiterate that the envisioned approach (the Digital Fairness Act) is not proportionate to the scale of the problem and instead softer measures would be more appropriate.

section_path: Ecommerce Europe Reply to Digital Fairness Act Public Consultation

paragraphs.text: The Fitness Check from 2024 suggests that there are gaps in existing legislation and that there is a justification for legislative intervention. We disagree with that assessment and are of the view that we already have a strong legal framework to enable enforcers to deal with emerging issues, including those in the digital environment. In 2024, Ecommerce Europe, together with EuroCommerce and Independent Retail Europe (IRE) jointly commissioned professors Kühling and Sauerborn from Regensburg University to assess what are the most common practices occurring in our sector (full study here, press release here). Our study largely addresses the issues signalled before as well as saying that any legislative intervention should be sector specific and proportionate.

Arguably, some of the issues raised by the Fitness Check merit further attention and would benefit from clarification. For example, the case-by-case approach, where authorities assess each situation individually rather than applying rigid rules, does indeed pose difficulties for authorities on the way they should apply it in practice. But one should not ignore that the case-by-case approach is also the most useful instrument of consumer law to deal with newly emerging consumer issues. It is precisely because this approach does not prescribe specific outcomes that the UCPD remains responsive and future-proof. It can however be conceded that there is room for improvement, from which both businesses and enforcers could benefit from.

The claim that consumers are generally more vulnerable online can also be debated: consumers benefit from much more comprehensive information (including the ability to quickly compare products, price, and condition across different merchants) online than when they buy in the physical world.

evidence_quotes.quote: deceptive patterns (dark patterns)

paragraphs.text: While the concept of deceptive patterns (dark patterns) may manifest differently, there is a list of practices categorised as deceptive patterns under EU law (see list in Annex), as well as the corresponding legal provision(s) that address any perceived issues. We view it is unnecessary to introduce another level of legislation to address a problem that is already addressed via legislation.

evidence_quotes.quote: deceptive design patterns

paragraphs.text: For example, deceptive design patterns are already defined in the Digital Services Act. However, there remains a need for regulatory consistency and clearer guidance on how these rules interact with related

ECOM REPLY TO DFA PUBLIC CONSULTATION
24 October 2025
ECOMMERCE EUROPE ★★★

evidence_quotes.quote: dedicating a section to deceptive design patterns.
The draft guidelines acknowledge the general lack of a consistent definition of dark patterns and stress the interplay between the regulations.

paragraphs.text: frameworks such as the GDPR and consumer protection law. The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) recently published its draft guidelines on the Digital Services Act (DSA) and the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), dedicating a section to deceptive design patterns. The draft guidelines acknowledge the general lack of a consistent definition of dark patterns and stress the interplay between the regulations. For effective legal enforcement, deceptive design should be treated not as the harm itself, but as a means through which regulatory harms manifest, like privacy violations (under GDPR) or unfair commercial practices. Thus, each regulation ought to address deceptive design patterns in the context of its specific policy objective, ensuring that definitions and enforcement are proportionate and grounded in demonstrable privacy or consumer harm. Furthermore, policymakers should explicitly acknowledge that not all forms of digital influence fall under the scope of harmful practices; in fact, positive nudging can enhance the consumer experience and should be distinguished as a legitimate tool that supports informed decision-making. Examples include design features that remind a buyer to double-check their shipping address or highlight a clear benefit, such as a temporary sale or promotional price.

evidence_quotes.quote: Moreover, the issue of deceptive patterns is platform-agnostic extending beyond the digital environment.
limited-time only sales to create a fake sense of urgency,

paragraphs.text: Moreover, the issue of deceptive patterns is platform-agnostic extending beyond the digital environment. It is easy to identify practices that exist in the offline world and could be qualified as "dark patterns". Take for example, (1) stores which use the limited-time only sales to create a fake sense of urgency, (2) how essential products such as milk and bread are placed at the back of the store, forcing customers to walk past other items which they could be interested in buying, (3) bags of crisps which often full of air, but their design is meant to have them seem bigger and with more content than they really are. During the previous mandate policy makers repeatedly stated the DSA and DMA must be designed in a way where what is illegal offline must be illegal online. If that was the logic then, the inverse should be equally applicable now. The equal treatment of online and offline sales environments is essential to business neutrality and retail innovation. Companies operating in the retail space are often doing so in an omnichannel manner, meaning that it would suddenly become more difficult to operate a shop online than offline. At a time when innovation and increased economic output are coming businesses with digital components it is illogical to overly-regulate the digital components of a business, impeding innovation and competitiveness.

evidence_quotes.quote: "dark pattern" exists if its influence leads to a consumer decision that is not supported by their actual will.
"practices that materially distort or impair, either on purpose or in effect, the ability of recipients of the service to make autonomous and informed choices or decisions"

paragraphs.text: It is insufficient to merely classify a practice as a dark pattern due to the vague definition of the term. As the study by Kühling and Sauerborn states, "it is not easy to distinguish between acceptable influence and dark manipulation". However, one possible demarcation criterion is that a "dark pattern" exists if its influence leads to a consumer decision that is not supported by their actual will. Recital 67 of the Digital Services Act describes deceptive patterns as "practices that materially distort or impair, either on purpose or in effect, the ability of recipients of the service to make autonomous and informed choices or decisions". This description should be the de facto definition, and guidelines should be developed, or at least updated to reflect this.

evidence_quotes.quote: The personalisation of online advertising should not be seen in a negative light, but rather, as an important element of the digital economy.

Personalisation in digital advertising is an evolution of this long-standing practice, leveraging technology to make ads more relevant and valuable for consumers based on their individual tastes and not solely on their inherent attributes.

paragraphs.text: The ability for online merchants to personalise advertising is essential to their business. The personalisation of online advertising should not be seen in a negative light, but rather, as an important element of the digital economy. Advertising has always been about reaching the right audience, whether through TV, newspapers, or billboards. However, traditional advertising, having no means to personalise, typically have followed broad, common-denominator trends about personal characteristics like gender, age, and ethnicity. This can impart bias. Personalisation in digital advertising is an evolution of this long-standing practice, leveraging technology to make ads more relevant and valuable for consumers based on their individual tastes and not solely on their inherent attributes. Far from being a new or disruptive concept, consumers expect it and are already used to it. In fact, personalised advertising is more beneficial for both

evidence_quotes.quote: ads are shown to consumers who are likely to be interested in a product

paragraphs.text: parties when ads are shown to consumers who are likely to be interested in a product, rather than being distributed to as many uninterested consumers as possible.

evidence_quotes.quote: Getting personalised advertising does not mean that the consumer will automatically purchase the advertised product.
Most importantly, consumers already have significant control over their personal data and can easily adjust their settings to opt out of personalised advertising if they wish.

paragraphs.text: There is no antagonism between advertising needs of businesses and consumers. Consumers prefer advertising that solves their problems rather than irrelevant or random ads that contribute to ad fatigue (a dog owner would not be interested in ads for cat food). They benefit from advertising as a discovery tool, introducing consumers to products and services that align with their interests and needs, based on their previous experience online and preferences shared with merchants. Getting personalised advertising does not mean that the consumer will automatically purchase the advertised product. Most importantly, consumers already have significant control over their personal data and can easily adjust their settings to opt out of personalised advertising if they wish.

evidence_quotes.quote: personalisation is a key growth driver.
Personalised advertising enables them to make the most of limited resources
SMEs also tend to rely heavily on personalised advertising

paragraphs.text: For businesses such as SMEs, which make up 90% of companies in Europe, personalisation is a key growth driver. Online shops in Europe operate under very high financial pressure and must navigate increasing competition by third-country players. In this context, SMEs rely on cost-effective advertising to reach their target audience efficiently. Personalised advertising enables them to make the most of limited resources, ensuring that their campaigns generate real value by reaching potential customers who are more likely to be interested in their products. Merchants know best how to set up their stores to meet customer needs and should retain the freedom to design their user experience, just as physical retailers are free to decide on store layout and product placement without being required to justify these choices to customers. Equal treatment of online and offline sales environments is essential to business neutrality and retail innovation. Without this, these smaller businesses would struggle to compete, ultimately limiting EU competitiveness, market diversity, and consumer choice. A recent study by Allied for Startups suggests that there is up to €80 billion in additional turnover for EU SMEs is linked to personalised advertising, supporting 200,000–500,000 SME jobs. SMEs also tend to rely heavily on personalised advertising, dedicating up to three quarter of their digital advertising budget to it.

evidence_quotes.quote: empower consumers to make choices regarding tracking-based advertising models

paragraphs.text: During the previous mandate, the European Commission launched the Cookie Pledge initiative. The goal of it was to counter the "cookie fatigue" and empower consumers to make choices regarding tracking-based advertising models, as well as push the industry towards contextual advertising instead. Following strong push back from the industry, as well as the fact that the European Data Protection Board refused to endorse the Cookie Pledge, the Commission dropped the initiative. Given the context in which this debate is taking place as well as the type of instrument envisioned, we would strongly recommend against incorporating this topic into the DFA. It may be worth considering that alternative approaches.

evidence_quotes.quote: the issue of personalisation

paragraphs.text: An element strongly connected to this subject is that of data protection, making it a matter of fundamental rights. The Kühling/ Sauerborn Report suggests that "the creation of prohibitions must be skilfully handled, and the relevant rights and interests of all parties involved must be protected." Moreover, the Fitness Check addresses the issue of personalisation while recalling the existence of a robust legal framework.

evidence_quotes.quote: Prohibits the collection of personal data without a valid legal basis,
the collection and processing of certain sensitive data that would allow the exploitation of user vulnerabilities (Article 9 GDPR)

paragraphs.text: Prohibits the collection of personal data without a valid legal basis, strictly regulates data of vulnerable consumers such as minors and prohibits the collection and processing of certain sensitive data that would allow the exploitation of user vulnerabilities (Article 9 GDPR).

evidence_quotes.quote: profiling for direct marketing purposes

paragraphs.text: Importantly, data subjects have a right to object to the processing of their personal data under Article 21 GDPR, a right that becomes absolute in the context of profiling for direct marketing purposes

evidence_quotes.quote: the presentation of ads based on profiling using sensitive data
profiling using the personal data of minors

paragraphs.text: Article 26(3) prohibits the presentation of ads based on profiling using sensitive data and limits the risk that ads exploit vulnerabilities. Whereas Article 28(2) prohibits the presentation of ads based on profiling using the personal data of minors and aims to ensure a higher level of protection for minors on platforms.

Sets rules on the limitations imposed on "gatekeepers" on the cross-referencing of data between different services.

Considering everything said before, we could at the most support the development of guidelines that clarify how the various legal tools work with each other and in which contexts. Concerns about malpractice are not unreasonable, but the EU already has ample instruments to address perceived consumer harms. The consumer protection framework already provides a broad range of potent tools for the Commission and national authorities to investigate and intervene where consumer harm is identified or anticipated.

evidence_quotes.quote: deceptive design

paragraphs.text: Online service providers continuously invest in safety, transparency, and usability to empower consumers and enhance the online experience. At the same time, it is important to recognise that in an online context, especially when more and more engagement is done via mobile phones, space is by definition constrained, which requires trade-offs in how information is presented, and choices are framed. Factoring into this the fact of inherent cognitive limits in humans, the conclusion emerges that it is not possible to put in place an entirely neutral online choice architecture. It is also worth highlighting that any discussion around deceptive design would have to address the issue of where to draw the line between legitimate marketing or sales practices that become manipulative or deceptive. Online services are a diverse ecosystem that are usually the primary touchpoints for consumers. Interactive features that enhance consumer engagement (such as chat functions, polls or live feedback) are essential components of modern digital shopping experiences and should not be conflated with manipulative design. Their use should remain permissible where information is clear, and choices remain autonomous. In fact, these features provide greater transparency when engagement allows buyers to ask questions of sellers and other past buyers. We believe that it is online services which should be able to determine how they interact with their customers, within the limits set by the applicable legal framework.

evidence_quotes.quote: avoid veering into the territory of product design
an overly granular and prescriptive approach to service layout or mandating specific colours or font sizes

paragraphs.text: In addition, when examining these issues, policymakers should consider how the applicable legal framework can encourage innovation and respect the fundamental freedom to conduct a business. Legislation should avoid veering into the territory of product design, for example by adopting an overly granular and prescriptive approach to service layout or mandating specific colours or font sizes. The diversity of service features, business models and the ability of companies to differentiate their services is necessary to allow a dynamic, innovative ecosystem.

evidence_quotes.quote: overly prescriptive approach on issues such as cookie banners
overly prescriptive \"withdrawal button\" (ultimately rejected), which would have moved away from a principle-based, appropriately flexible \"withdrawal function\"

paragraphs.text: For example, we have seen regulators take an overly prescriptive approach on issues such as cookie banners, that have resulted in a negative experience for consumers (as was recognised by the European Commission in the proposed Cookie Pledge). Another example comes from the Distance Marketing of Financial Services Directive and the provisions on an overly prescriptive "withdrawal button" (ultimately rejected), which would have moved away from a principle-based, appropriately flexible "withdrawal function"

evidence_quotes.quote: into mandating a specific design choice.

paragraphs.text: into mandating a specific design choice. Legislators should not view any one right as an absolute, but weigh different rights against one another, if we are to achieve a balanced regulatory outcome.

We are supportive of the European Commission’s work in this space and have been actively providing feedback on pricing matters for many years. Our members support the aim of ensuring that consumers are provided with meaningful price information. Yet, it is evident that diverging transpositions, and interpretations have led to complications for companies and national authorities alike. Particularly, this is the case for the Price Indication Directive (PID) and the introduction of Article 6a via the Omnibus Directive.

evidence_quotes.quote: indication of the selling price and the price per unit of measurement of products offered by traders to consumers

paragraphs.text: The approach taken by certain Member States, including Belgium, France, Poland and Portugal, of extending the scope of the PID to include services may infringe EU law. From Article 1 of the PID it is clear that this Directive only applies to products: "The purpose of this Directive is to stipulate indication of the selling price and the price per unit of measurement of products offered by traders to consumers in order to improve consumer information and to facilitate comparison of prices." The Commission’s guidance on Article 6a PID confirms this by stating that Article 6a “does not apply to services (including digital services) or to digital content”. We find it encouraging that Commission has notified both Belgium and Portugal about the risk of infringement procedures, but we would like to see more prompt actions.

evidence_quotes.quote: provisions that address price comparisons that may mislead consumers.
restrictions on certain aspects of price comparisons, prohibiting the use of crossed-out prices and the display of value or percentage discounts in such comparisons.

paragraphs.text: Regarding price comparisons, this is already governed by existing regulation within the European Union. Article 6 (Misleading actions) of the UCPD serves as the cornerstone for the regulation of price comparisons. This article includes rules and guidelines aimed at preventing deceptive or unfair commercial practices and, notably, provisions that address price comparisons that may mislead consumers. However, despite the established framework provided by the UCPD, several Member States have imposed restrictions on certain aspects of price comparisons, prohibiting the use of crossed-out prices and the display of value or percentage discounts in such comparisons.

Retailers face difficulties across Member States due to the diverse and, at times, diverging interpretations and applications of regulations by national authorities across different EU countries. The lack of consistent enforcement of regulations raises questions about equal treatment and the overarching principles of the EU's Single Market.
Update and ensure that the Commission’s guidance allows traders to provide consumers with meaningful information and limit the possibility for diverging interpretation at national level.

evidence_quotes.quote: Focus the guidance on the accountability of companies responsible for using price comparisons, addressing issues of integrity, fairness, and the need for clear justifications of the claims made.

paragraphs.text: Focus the guidance on the accountability of companies responsible for using price comparisons, addressing issues of integrity, fairness, and the need for clear justifications of the claims made.
Pursue infringement cases against Member States which have not transposed the Directive properly.

evidence_quotes.quote: Clarify that traders may compare their prices against other prices
the price comparison is not misleading.

paragraphs.text: Clarify that traders may compare their prices against other prices, both as standalone comparisons and in conjunction with other comparisons (whether to the prior price, where applicable, or other reference prices), provided that the nature of the price comparison is clearly stated, and the price comparison is not misleading.
Streamline the list of exempted products and establish a common understanding of which products qualify for exemption, when and for how long in the Commission’s guidance.
Creating a code of conduct on price advantages under Article 10 UCPD.

evidence_quotes.quote: including personalisation/customisation

paragraphs.text: When considering unfair marketing practices in relation to pricing, it is important to consider the varied and legitimate factors that influence price setting in marketplaces. Variable pricing can arise for many valid reasons, including personalisation/customisation, volume discounts, or buyer-driven choices such as shipping speed. Reference pricing should therefore be reasonably accurate but flexible enough to reflect that prices are not static. This flexibility is especially important in marketing contexts where consumers are guided toward affordable choices (e.g., “stemware under 50 Euros” or “Mother’s Day jewellery starting at 30 Euros”).

evidence_quotes.quote: time-limited offers and auctions, whether presented in real time or otherwise, are long-standing, legitimate marketing techniques used in both online and offline shopping.
They allow traders to respond flexibly to demand and consumer interest, and should not be presumed manipulative when the offer terms are clear and the consumer retains full choice.

paragraphs.text: In addition, time-limited offers and auctions, whether presented in real time or otherwise, are long-standing, legitimate marketing techniques used in both online and offline shopping. When transparently disclosed they should not be presumed manipulative. They allow traders to respond flexibly to demand and consumer interest, and should not be presumed manipulative when the offer terms are clear and the consumer retains full choice.

evidence_quotes.quote: drip pricing, also known as “headline price”

paragraphs.text: On drip pricing, also known as “headline price”, requirements should be aligned with the consumer’s actual purchasing journey. The final price a consumer pays, including all mandatory charges, typically depends on the contents of the full basket, which can only be calculated once the consumer has made all relevant selections. Delivery or service fees, for example, should only need to be displayed once the basket has been calculated, ensuring that the consumer sees a clear and complete total price at the basket level.

evidence_quotes.quote: the final price cannot reasonably be calculated in advance, due to optional extras or customisation.
dynamic dropdown menu to update prices automatically as buyers select options (such as size, material, or personalisation features)
base “headline price” reflecting the lowest-priced option.
far more accurate and consumer-friendly than requiring all possible prices to be shown upfront.

paragraphs.text: At the same time, we would welcome if the Commission would provide practical guidance on how to display prices when the final price cannot reasonably be calculated in advance, due to optional extras or customisation. For instance, it should be considered acceptable for a dynamic dropdown menu to update prices automatically as buyers select options (such as size, material, or personalisation features), with the base “headline price” reflecting the lowest-priced option. For marketplaces with a large share of highly customisable or made-to-order products, this approach is far more accurate and consumer-friendly than requiring all possible prices to be shown upfront.

evidence_quotes.quote: display a clear base product price exclusive of delivery or extras, and then prominently show the total price at the basket stage would ensure both transparency and usability.

A hardline requirement to display an all-inclusive total price in each and every invitation to purchase, with very few and narrow exceptions, will result in an overload of information for consumers that risks reducing price transparency.

paragraphs.text: Overall, consumers benefit most from accurate pricing at the point where it can reasonably be calculated. A flexible approach where platforms display a clear base product price exclusive of delivery or extras, and then prominently show the total price at the basket stage would ensure both transparency and usability. Overly rigid requirements would risk making product pages cluttered and confusing, ultimately undermining consumer understanding. Our members already invest heavily in their customer journey to ensure the user experience is as smooth as possible. A hardline requirement to display an all-inclusive total price in each and every invitation to purchase, with very few and narrow exceptions, will result in an overload of information for consumers that risks reducing price transparency. At the end of the day, we would like to avoid the scenario where our members would need to alter well-established user interfaces in ways that provide no discernible consumer benefit and, in some cases, result in confusing and misleading information overload.

In a competitive marketplace, businesses are incentivised to innovate and improve their transparency to attract and retain customers. This dynamic naturally discourages practices that are not consumer-friendly, as such actions would lead to a loss of customers to competitors.

evidence_quotes.quote: digital subscriptions

paragraphs.text: The rapid expansion of digital subscriptions and online contract models across the European market has created both opportunities and challenges for businesses and consumers. Whereas legislative efforts

evidence_quotes.quote: The diversity of subscription models, from flexible "soft" subscriptions for physical goods to digital service contracts, underscores the importance of rules that protect consumers without hindering innovation or imposing undue burdens on businesses.

paragraphs.text: intensify to safeguard consumer rights, particularly in the areas of pre-sale disclosures, contract transparency, and the simplicity of cancellations. We underline though that there has to be a nuanced and proportionate approach. The diversity of subscription models, from flexible "soft" subscriptions for physical goods to digital service contracts, underscores the importance of rules that protect consumers without hindering innovation or imposing undue burdens on businesses.

evidence_quotes.quote: reminders can be a suitable tool to help consumers keep track of their active subscriptions.
However, in some cases, we believe it is unnecessary to send a reminder before each automatic renewal.
We therefore request that physical goods subscriptions be excluded from reminder notice requirements.

paragraphs.text: As for notification requirements, reminders can be a suitable tool to help consumers keep track of their active subscriptions. However, in some cases, we believe it is unnecessary to send a reminder before each automatic renewal. For example, in the case of physical goods, the mere delivery already serves as a reminder. Customers are highly unlikely to forget about an active subscription when they receive regular physical deliveries. A recent study conducted by Dr. Christoph Busch also recognises that the subscription-based economy is very diverse and particularly in the case of physical deliveries, such subscriptions generally carry low risks since regular deliveries remind consumers of their commitment. We therefore request that physical goods subscriptions be excluded from reminder notice requirements.

evidence_quotes.quote: In connection with sales with a negative option feature that do not involve the automatic delivery of physical goods, it is a violation of this Rule and an unfair act or practice in violation of Section 5 of the FTC Act for a negative option seller to fail to provide consumers reminders, at least annually, identifying the product or service, the frequency and amount of charges, and the means to cancel.

paragraphs.text: For example, the US FTC proposed such an exclusion in the draft Negative Option Rule before reminder notices were removed entirely from the draft for the final rule. ("In connection with sales with a negative option feature that do not involve the automatic delivery of physical goods, it is a violation of this Rule and an unfair act or practice in violation of Section 5 of the FTC Act for a negative option seller to fail to provide consumers reminders, at least annually, identifying the product or service, the frequency and amount of charges, and the means to cancel.")

evidence_quotes.quote: reminder notices could be an appropriate measure for subscription contracts that automatically renew for long periods (bi-annually or annually)
requiring frequent reminders for short-term contracts that renew weekly or monthly would overwhelm consumers
reminders only every six months for active subscriptions or when a subscription automatically renews for six months

paragraphs.text: In the case of short-term contracts, while we believe that reminder notices could be an appropriate measure for subscription contracts that automatically renew for long periods (bi-annually or annually), requiring frequent reminders for short-term contracts that renew weekly or monthly would overwhelm consumers. It needs to be remembered that consumers today usually have multiple subscriptions, and the number of reminder notices could become spam-like. Short-term models also pose significantly less risk due to the flexibility to cancel the subscription for the following week or month. The UK has adopted an approach, requiring businesses to send reminders only every six months for active subscriptions or when a subscription automatically renews for six months ("Where a trader enters into a subscription contract with a consumer ... the trader must give to the consumer a notice (referred to in this Chapter as a "reminder notice") in respect of each renewal payment that relates to the end of a relevant six-month period. (2)A "relevant six-month period" for the purposes of subsection (1) is—(a) the period of 6 months beginning with the day after the day on which the contract was entered into, and (b) each subsequent period of 6 months beginning with the day after the day on which the consumer last became liable for a renewal payment in respect of which a reminder notice was required under subsection").

evidence_quotes.quote: terms of free trials or introductory offers
a free trial followed by a discounted ongoing subscription, which is only available if they had subscribed by a specific date

paragraphs.text: Firstly, we believe this requirement is generally not needed, as providing pre-contractual information about the terms of free trials or introductory offers should suffice to inform customers adequately. It is also important to consider the high risk of customers missing an opt-in request, which could lead to unintended consequences, i.e. an unintended end of the contract. This could lead to situations where a customer would lose access to their account and data, missing a desired delivery (such as food or household items they rely on), or forfeiting a special discount offer (for example, a customer might lose the benefit of a free trial followed by a discounted ongoing subscription, which is only available if they had subscribed by a specific date).

ECOMMERCE EUROPE

evidence_quotes.quote: introductory offers should generally be excluded from opt-in (or reminder requirements)
a free trial that turns into an ongoing subscription

paragraphs.text: Secondly, introductory offers should generally be excluded from opt-in (or reminder requirements), as customers are already paying for these subscriptions and are therefore naturally more aware of them. This contrasts with free trials, where customers are more likely to forget about a free trial that turns into an ongoing subscription since no fees are being charged.

evidence_quotes.quote: free trial or introductory offer turns into an ongoing subscription.
for contracts that renew weekly or monthly, the risk to consumers is minimal.

paragraphs.text: Thirdly, it should also be considered to exclude physical products and short-term models from opt-in (and reminder) requirements when a free trial or introductory offer turns into an ongoing subscription. The delivery of physical goods serves as a regular reminder, making it highly unlikely for customers to forget about their subscription. Additionally, for contracts that renew weekly or monthly, the risk to consumers is minimal. Even if they overlook the contract rollover, they can cancel in a timely manner.

evidence_quotes.quote: The offering of free trials is a costly marketing measure for companies to attract new customers which takes different forms (for example demos, free month, or other benefits).

paragraphs.text: The offering of free trials is a costly marketing measure for companies to attract new customers which takes different forms (for example demos, free month, or other benefits). Free trials also empower SMEs to expand their customer base. We would therefore caution against further complicating this commercial technique.

evidence_quotes.quote: contract cancellations and subscriptions,
digital contracts and subscriptions that consumers conclude online.

paragraphs.text: When referring to contract cancellations and subscriptions, we would argue that the existing legislation already tackles them. This aspect is acknowledged even by the Fitness Check Report, stating that that EU consumer law fully applies to digital contracts and subscriptions that consumers conclude online.

evidence_quotes.quote: a prominent and easily legible withdrawal function

paragraphs.text: Article 11a CRD includes a requirement for traders to provide a prominent and easily legible withdrawal function

evidence_quotes.quote: as easy to unsubscribe/cancel as it is to subscribe
barriers to contract termination as aggressive practices.

paragraphs.text: In the UCPD Guidance, the Commission considered that it should be as easy to unsubscribe/cancel as it is to subscribe, deriving this principle from the interpretation of Article 9(d) UCPD, which considers barriers to contract termination as aggressive practices.

evidence_quotes.quote: disproportionate general conditions for terminating the provision of a core platform service.

paragraphs.text: In addition, the DMA states that a gatekeeper should not apply disproportionate general conditions for terminating the provision of a core platform service.

evidence_quotes.quote: explicit information about the payable charges

paragraphs.text: Existing information obligations in the CRD, UCTD and UCPD. Art. 8(2) CRD specifically requires the trader to remind the consumer about the 'obligation to pay' immediately before concluding the online contract, and the Payment Services Directive requires explicit information about the payable charges and the consumer's consent before payment.

evidence_quotes.quote: problematic subscription practices

paragraphs.text: Thus, instead of creating new legislative layers, the Commission should consider developing case-study based guidance. Such guidance could provide detailed, real-world examples of problematic subscription practices, which would then be tested in different scenarios to assess how existing consumer law would apply to them.

evidence_quotes.quote: Excessive reminders might lead to confusion for consumers who would receive numerous reminders for each subscription.

paragraphs.text: Excessive reminders might lead to confusion for consumers who would receive numerous reminders for each subscription.

A cancellation button would not be appropriate for all types of consumer contracts.

paragraphs.text: A cancellation button would not be appropriate for all types of consumer contracts. For instance, requiring a cancellation button can be inappropriate for digital goods with immediate access, such as software. Similarly, subscription services for the actual delivery of goods may face challenges with cancellation buttons.

paragraphs.text: The introduction of a cancellation button also raises concerns in relation to logistics management and the possibility of abuse by consumers.

paragraphs.text: Such a cancellation button carries risks associated with misuse by bad actors.

evidence_quotes.quote: concluding a subscription contract typically involves multiple steps
the process of terminating a subscription as straightforward and easy as the process for subscribing to it

paragraphs.text: It should generally be noted that concluding a subscription contract typically involves multiple steps, such as creating an account, providing contact details, entering payment information, and selecting a suitable subscription offer. In most cases, consumers are also required to verify their contact information (via email or phone). This should be considered when developing a policy solution that ensures that businesses make the process of terminating a subscription as straightforward and easy as the process for subscribing to it.

section_path: Establishing requirements for automatic subscription renewal reminders should be proportionate and flexible.

evidence_quotes.quote: We agree that contracts concluded online should also be cancellable online.

The UK Digital Markets, Competition, and Consumer Act (DMCCA) requires that consumers must be able to end a subscription contract in a way that is straightforward and without taking steps that are not reasonably necessary to complete the cancellation.
We find this reasonable and fair since many consumers actively seek discounts when cancelling.

paragraphs.text: We agree that contracts concluded online should also be cancellable online. However, we believe this should not be required in an overly prescriptive manner. The UK Digital Markets, Competition, and Consumer Act (DMCCA) requires that consumers must be able to end a subscription contract in a way that is straightforward and without taking steps that are not reasonably necessary to complete the cancellation. The UK DMCCA does not prohibit traders from making offers or seeking feedback from consumers during the exit mechanism, provided that the arrangements for exiting a contract comply with the DMCCA's requirements. Accordingly, offers or feedback must not frustrate or unreasonably elongate the exit process. We find this reasonable and fair since many consumers actively seek discounts when cancelling.

It is important to inform the customer about this fact before he continues with the cancellation.

evidence_quotes.quote: These include provisions for clearly labelled advertising, bans on hidden advertising and transparency requirements for posts, incentivised content and endorsements.

paragraphs.text: There's already a comprehensive legislative framework relevant for influencers and the platforms that host their content: the DSA (Article 26), the AVMSD (Articles 9 & 28), the UCPD, the UCTD, the Consumer Rights Directive and the Digital Content Directive. These include provisions for clearly labelled advertising, bans on hidden advertising and transparency requirements for posts, incentivised content and endorsements. These rules apply regardless of where a sale is initiated or promoted, ensuring that illegal products or misleading marketing practices can be tackled whether they take place via social platforms or more conventional e-commerce channels. Given this robust framework, we see no need for additional legislation targeting influencer marketing or social commerce within the Digital Fairness Act.

section_path: Establishing requirements for automatic subscription renewal reminders should be proportionate and flexible.

paragraphs.text: Better practical guidance and increasing customer awareness would be more effective than introducing new legislation. We welcome initiatives such as the European Commission's Influencer Legal Hub, as a great resource to provide clarity on applicable rules. The current framework already covers key issues including advertising disclosure requirements, problematic content, and protection of children.

paragraphs.text: Nowadays, it is still very difficult for brands to control the impacts of an influencer among its own followers. That is because current tools do not offer enough assurance of what kind of public is being impacted; and even less among the rest of users because there is no way for a brand to detect whether the content promoted by an influencer will end up being shown to other public outside of its followers

paragraphs.text: We do not perceive the validity of the justification for intervening via legislation in this area. At this moment the usage of automated contracting is very scarce, and it would be extremely premature to intervene in an area that has yet to even take shape.

While we place great value on the academic debate surrounding the subject, we do not see the need for legal intervention. It would be much more advisable to allow the current situation to evolve some more, observe technological developments and then consider intervening via legal or non-legal instruments. We sense that the focus at this moment is mainly directed at theoretical issues that might happen. The argument for increased legal certainty is also insufficient as innovative companies are experimenting with different tools no matter what. For example, companies existed in the AI space even before the AI Act. The absence of the AI Act did not prevent the emergence of ChatGPT.

evidence_quotes.quote: one particular example here is the right of withdrawal.
Article 9 of the Consumer Rights Directive enables consumers withdraw from distance or off-premises contracts without any reason.

paragraphs.text: As for the consumer angle, they are already well protected in cases where the automated system may not work as intended. There are numerous consumer protection rules, many of which have been repeatedly listed in this paper. However, one particular example here is the right of withdrawal. Article 9 of the Consumer Rights Directive enables consumers withdraw from distance or off-premises contracts without any reason.

ECOM REPLY TO DFA PUBLIC CONSULTATION
24 October 2025
ECOMMERCE EUROPE

Considering the above, the case for legislating on automated contracting should not be a subject of the present but a matter of the future. Legislating in an area that has yet to even emerge properly does not constitute simplification or reduction of burdens.

Instead of spending time to create a new law that simply expands the list of other laws we already have, it would be more productive to create synergies and clarify interactions between laws, develop guidelines explaining how the different existing rules work in practice, and give regulators tools to properly enforce the existing framework towards all players active in the EU territory.

One of the key complaints of regulators and companies in the digital fairness fitness check is lack the harmonised interpretations as well as the uncertainty associated with the UCPD's case-by-case approach. Another layer of laws will not fix these problems. It will merely pile up into the stack of other laws. To improve implementation, it would be more beneficial if the EC would take an active role in clarifying the way in which the existing rules work in concert with the other laws/ guidelines developed the years before. For example, the Commission could:
Clarify application of UCPD Article 12, taking into account sales channels, company size, customer type, and interface design.
Strengthen enforcement by creating a shared database of cases, precedents, and guidance.
Encourage regular dialogue between regulators and industry to build consistent interpretations.

These proposals would have a two-fold effect. On the one hand, the Commission could develop closer cooperation between authorities to reach similar conclusions and embrace comparable interpretations thus promoting a more unified Single Market. On the other hand, this will have the much-desired simplification effect. Simplification of laws does not have to be achieved by adopting new laws, but rather by clarifying how they work with each other. One should not fight fire with fire.

evidence_quotes.quote: the difficulty to enforce existing regulations towards rogue and/or extra-EU actors.
whether this would be via better coordination through the CPC reform project, or by speeding up procedures,

paragraphs.text: One of the key issues surrounding this discussion is the difficulty to enforce existing regulations towards rogue and/or extra-EU actors. Therefore, it would be useful to provide authorities with additional procedure tools. The bigger question then is whether this would be via better coordination through the CPC reform project, or by speeding up procedures, to mitigate the risk of seeing some actors eluding regulation and buying time by playing EU fragmentation.

We would be in favour of a softer approach that would fix the problems listed in the Report. To begin with, the Consumer Protection Cooperation (CPC) Network is a prime avenue for exchanging best practices between authorities and improving the interpretation of existing laws. We also support the possibility of the Commission gaining some enforcement powers of consumer law, in specific and limited circumstances.

evidence_quotes.quote: the creation of a voluntary reporting system where businesses could flag (anonymously) to the regulators the areas where they face challenges in complying with digital fairness rules.

paragraphs.text: The Commission could also consider making better use of voluntary and self-regulatory systems. For example, the creation of a voluntary reporting system where businesses could flag (anonymously) to the regulators the areas where they face challenges in complying with digital fairness rules. Such a system could benefit both businesses and regulators to better understand which are the enforcement gaps and what would be the best ways to address them.

The Commission should also consider developing case-study based guidance. The guidance could provide detailed, real-world examples of problematic digital practices which would then be tested in different scenarios to assess how existing consumer law would apply to them. Enforcement authorities would benefit

ECOMMERCE EUROPE
from increased clarity on how the provisions are to be interpreted and applied, whereas businesses would find it easier to ensure compliance by design.

evidence_quotes.quote: misleading practices online

paragraphs.text: The Commission should advocate and invest in increasing consumer awareness. The Fitness Check points out that 23% of European citizens still feel they lack knowledge about their rights in the digital environment. By increasing user knowledge, the European Union would encourage digital players to offer safer, fairer and more reliable services: the more consumers are aware of misleading practices online, the more companies will need to stand out positively by eradicating these practices from their sites. This virtuous effect requires that consumers retain the possibility of exercising their freedom of choice in competitive digital markets. The implementation of the Digital Market Act is therefore essential to guarantee this ability to change supplier, particularly for consumers using "gatekeeper" services who risk being more exposed to unfair practices.

One of the core findings of both the Kühling/ Sauerborn Report as well as the Commission's Fitness Check is that there is a lack of enforcement. On the Commission side, the Report clearly states that "the effectiveness of the three Directives is undermined by sub optimal private and public enforcement, in line with findings from previous evaluations". The document also singles out the lack of case law concerning the application of the laws in the context of novel and data-driven practices. There is a lack in the Member States of quantitative data and statistics to distinguish between online and offline scenarios. Therefore, the authors of the Report chose to focus only on the landmark decisions regarding the problematic practices.

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: In response to the Commission's findings, we perceive the lack of case law or of data to be hardly a justification for more law. In this specific instance, it would be more appropriate for the Commission to enter into a dialogue with national authorities, national courts and judges to better understand what the hurdles are, how to overcome them and why is there this level of reluctance among authorities to initiate court cases. Whereas for the Kühling/ Sauerborn Report, it also talks about insufficient enforcement as a cause for the perceived deficits of the current consumer legal framework. Specifically, regarding dark patterns, the study recommends enhancing enforcement actions to ensure their effectiveness. The issues connected to enforcement may be due to lack of resources, staffing or different enforcement priorities in the Member States.

In relation to third-country traders, it could be argued that it is an area which could benefit from clarifications. The current level playing field debate brings attention to this issue and may warrant adjustments to properly address it.

Prior to undergoing substantial legislative changes, the Commission could enter into regulatory dialogues with the authorities in the Member States. The CPC Network provides the ideal forum for authorities and Commission officials to discuss issues and what would be the best ways to address the current deficiencies, via ways other than legislative changes.

evidence_quotes.quote: harmonised interpretations could reduce the friction between authorities of different Member States
may lead to cross-border enforcement actions when the goals are aligned.

paragraphs.text: Besides this, harmonised interpretations could reduce the friction between authorities of different Member States and may lead to cross-border enforcement actions when the goals are aligned.

For our final remarks, we would like to underline that Ecommerce Europe firmly believes that the introduction of a new legislative instrument, such as the Digital Fairness Act, is neither necessary nor proportionate. The EU already possesses a mature and comprehensive body of consumer and digital law capable of addressing the practices identified in the Fitness Check, including the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive, the Consumer Rights Directive, the GDPR, the Digital Services Act, and the Digital
in
www.ecommerce-europe.eu | info@ecommerce-europe.eu | +32 (0) 496 273 060
Rue du Commerce 31, 1000 Brussels (Belgium)
ECOM REPLY TO DFA PUBLIC CONSULTATION
24 October 2025
ECOMMERCE EUROPE
Markets Act. The recurring problem is not a lack of rules, but the uneven and inconsistent enforcement of existing ones.

A new layer of legislation would only deepen fragmentation, increase compliance burdens, and create further uncertainty for both enforcers and businesses. Rather than legislating, the Commission should focus on:
Improving enforcement and coordination through the CPC Network, harmonise guidance, exchange best practices and develop advanced digital tools.
Clarifying existing provisions by developing targeted, case-based guidance.
Promoting simplification by ensuring that different EU instruments operate coherently and consistently.
Encouraging voluntary initiatives and dialogue with businesses to identify compliance challenges and good practices.
Strengthening consumer awareness and supporting enforcement capacity at national level instead of multiplying legislative acts.

The Fitness Check, as well as the conclusions of the Draghi and Letta reports, point in the same direction: the Single Market needs a period of consolidation, not expansion, of its digital rulebook. The goal should be to make existing laws work better, not to create new ones that risk overlapping with what is already in place. A clear, coherent, and enforceable framework will deliver more fairness and trust in the digital environment than another legislative act ever could.
